data.source: Laboratory experiment with behavioral tasks (moral dilemmas, lexical decision task) and questionnaires
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant responses at the trial level (aggregated to participant-level proportions/means for analyses)
data.notes: Participants were Korean college students screened for prior exposure to moral dilemma literature and advanced psychology courses. Mixed-design with social observation manipulation (observed vs. control). Tasks implemented using Cogent 2000 in MATLAB (v8.4.0). Pretest used process dissociation (PD) dilemmas to estimate deontological (D) and utilitarian (U) parameters. Main task included 50 dilemmas (20 personal, 15 impersonal, 15 non-moral). Binary appropriateness judgments followed by 4-point confidence ratings; response keys counterbalanced. Lexical decision task used a Go/no-go format with categories: Reputation, Control, Warmth, Competence, and Non-words. Post-experiment questionnaires: IRI, Need for Cognition, Fear of Negative Evaluation; mood and arousal pre/post.

method.description: A mixed-design behavioral experiment tested whether social observation (as a proxy for reputation concern) biases moral judgments toward deontology. Participants first completed a pretest to estimate baseline deontological and utilitarian inclinations using process dissociation dilemmas. They were then randomly assigned to an observed (two putative observers monitoring) or control condition (anonymized responses) and completed a main moral dilemma task (personal, impersonal, non-moral). Afterward, participants performed a Go/no-go lexical decision task targeting reputation-related constructs (warmth/competence), followed by personality and state questionnaires. Statistical analyses assessed effects of group and dilemma type on deontological response proportion, confidence, and reaction times, and group-by-word-category effects on lexical decision RTs.
steps: Recruit 48 Korean college participants; obtain consent and collect baseline mood/arousal.
Pretest: Administer 20 PD moral dilemmas to estimate baseline deontological (D) and utilitarian (U) parameters; record binary appropriateness decisions and 4-point confidence.
Randomly assign participants to Observed (OBS) or Control (CON) group; implement social observation manipulation (OBS: two observers with monitoring device; CON: anonymity and no monitoring).

Main task: Present 50 dilemmas (20 personal moral, 15 impersonal moral, 15 non-moral) in three-screen format; collect binary appropriateness judgments and confidence ratings; counterbalance response keys; record RTs.
Lexical decision task (Go/no-go): Present two-letter Korean strings; respond to real words; include categories Reputation, Control, Warmth, Competence, and Non-words; record accuracy and RTs.
Post-experiment: Administer IRI, Need for Cognition, Fear of Negative Evaluation; re-measure mood/arousal.

Statistical analysis in SPSS: Mixed ANOVAs and t-tests for group × dilemma/response effects on deontological proportion, confidence, RTs; analyze LDT RTs with group × word category; report p-values, partial eta-squared, Cohen’s d.
method.models: Mixed-design ANOVAs (between-subjects: group; within-subjects: dilemma type; response type); Bonferroni-corrected paired t-tests for simple effects; Process Dissociation analysis for D and U parameters; exploratory Pearson correlations; Greenhouse-Geisser correction where applicable. Effect sizes: partial eta-squared (ηp²), Cohen’s d.
method.outcome_variable: Primary: Proportion of deontological responses in moral dilemmas. Secondary: Decision confidence ratings; Reaction times to moral dilemma judgments; Lexical decision task reaction times (by word category) and Warmth/Competence RT ratio.
method.independent_variables: Group (Observed vs. Control); Dilemma type (Personal vs. Impersonal vs. Non-moral); Response type (Appropriate/utilitarian vs. Inappropriate/deontological); In some analyses, baseline deontological parameter (D) included as covariate.
method.control_variables: Sex; motor key counterbalancing; baseline D and U parameters (from PD pretest); personality measures (IRI, Need for Cognition, Fear of Negative Evaluation) for intergroup equivalence; mood and arousal pre/post.
method.tools_software: MATLAB 8.4.0 with Cogent 2000 (task implementation); SPSS 23.0 (statistical analyses); G*Power 3.1 (a priori power analysis).

results.summary: Social observation increased deontological judgments specifically in personal moral dilemmas, without baseline differences in moral orientation or personality traits. Observed participants reported lower decision confidence overall and exhibited longer reaction times across dilemmas. In personal dilemmas, utilitarian choices were slower than deontological ones. A three-way pattern suggested that, under observation, faster responders showed stronger deontological bias in moral (but not non-moral) dilemmas. In the lexical decision task, observation selectively facilitated responses to warmth-related trait words and reduced responses to competence-related words, yielding a lower Warmth/Competence RT ratio in the observed group. Mood and arousal did not differ pre/post by group.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Group × Dilemma type interaction on deontological proportion
Main effect of Group on decision confidence (OBS − CON)
Main effect of Group on RT (OBS − CON)

metadata.original_paper_title: Social observation increases deontological judgments in moral dilemmas
